title: What Lies Beneath - A game book of solo dungeon-diving, dice, decisions and death!
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A9RXB8YTc6Y

Daniel (@dungeondive) examines What Lies Beneath, a solo dungeon-crawl gamebook by Chris Scaffidi illustrated by channel favourite Jason Glover (a review copy) — boasting 13 win endings and dozens of deaths. You wake amnesiac in a crypt, a leathery tumour spreading on your arm, and must escape, recover your identity, and cure the disease. He likens it to Warlock of Firetop Mountain but considerably richer in choices.

Character creation rolls a class (warrior, druid and more) with hit/dex/wit/vigor stats, and the tests are a varied bag of mini-games: combat (a dice pool against a target), dexterity (a flick-a-die tower-tapping game, with a no-flick option for travel), and wit (roll dice then spend wit to manipulate them into a straight). You track Karma and Ward tags plus item codes resolved via an Arcana appendix. The standout system is XP carryover — points earned by a slain character are spent to buff your *next* one, softening the genre's brutal dead-ends.

What he prizes most is that nearly every passage offers three or four real options, sparing you the locked-out feeling of pickier gamebooks — a modern, mechanically generous twist on the Fighting Fantasy nostalgia, with Glover's art throughout.

----

Does an XP-carryover system that strengthens your next character make a deadly gamebook more appealing, or do you prefer the clean-slate stakes of permadeath each run?
